label: macro
text: This study demonstrates an increasing, dose dependent in vitro efficacy of UVC for bacterial growth inhibition against clinical isolates when using a prototype device emitting a 23 mm diameter beam with a wavelength of 265 nm. Whilst complete inhibition was not achieved for all isolates, good antimicrobial efficacy was seen for the majority of isolates. In particular, bacteria that present increased challenges for conventional antibiotics, such as MDR‐MRSP and  P. aeruginosa  isolates exhibited vast reduction in bacterial survival after 5 s of exposure. This demonstrates a clear potential for UVC as an adjunctive therapy, with the aim to reduce duration of topical antibiotic therapy and improve clinical resolution of infections; however, further controlled in vivo efficacy and safety studies are warranted. From a clinical perspective, this prototype device provides a practical approach to UVC delivery, with a large beam diameter that allows treatment of the whole corneal surface in a single short exposure from an applicable working distance. Clinical trials and safety studies in companion animals are warranted to assess whether these antimicrobial effects can be demonstrated in vivo and to ensure UVC light therapy is safe.

label: macro
text: Breast cancer (BC) is most prevalent in females but also accounts for <1% of male cancer cases and 0.2% of male cancer‐related deaths. Distribution of histological subtypes, receptor status, and age of diagnosis varies based on sex, and a growing body of evidence supports sex‐specific molecular differences in BC. However, this is limited by the smaller number of male cases available for study compared to the thousands of cases of female BC. We combined publicly available male BC gene expression datasets for 195 patients from 4 studies and split randomly into discovery and validation sets. Clustering and gene expression analysis were performed. Two stable clusters were identified initially, confirmed in the validation set. Cluster C1 was enriched for genes associated with MAPK signalling and arylesterase activity. Cluster C2 showed enrichment of genes associated with proliferation, invasion, and metastasis, along with enrichment of gene ontology and pathway terms related to ECM regulation, particularly collagen‐containing ECM. Of note, when stratified by ERα and PR status, no enrichment was observed with the predicted PAM50 classification. ERα and MAPK signalling were enriched in both clusters, albeit through different gene sets. Since these pathways were enriched, we investigated the signalling regulation of ERα based on immunohistochemical expression of phosphorylated ERα (S104, S118, S167, S294) and their prognostic roles. This analysis also revealed distinctions from female BC, showing a lack of prognostic outcome for any of these biomarkers. We show that male BC does not align with female BC in the same way that intrinsic subtypes of female BC are not identical. As BC heterogeneity is well recognised, we propose that male BC should be considered as a potentially unique clinical subtype of BC.

label: macro
text: In parallel, seasonal variation in biomarkers has attracted increasing interest in cardiometabolic medicine. Several traits relevant to cardiovascular and metabolic health, including body weight, blood pressure and glycemic indices, are known to vary by season, with consistent winter and summer differences described across populations. Moreover, the magnitude of within‐person variability in such traits has itself been linked to adverse outcomes, suggesting that the amplitude of fluctuation can carry clinically meaningful information. By comparison, seasonal patterns in liver enzymes have received limited attention, although Miyake and colleagues previously reported population‐level circannual fluctuations in AST and ALT in a large outpatient sample, with winter peaks and summer nadirs. Whether such variation also occurs at the level of individual patients, and whether it carries clinical relevance, has not been examined.

Frequent switching between Hindi, English and Punjabi required careful attention to cultural nuance, tone, idioms and emotion during transcription and translation. Translation was treated as an interpretive process central to meaning‐making and representation, consistent with qualitative research standards. Collaborative review by bilingual team members enhanced accuracy and rigour.

label: macro
text: The global incidence of cancer is growing rapidly (Sung et al., ), and in the UK, one in two people will now develop cancer in their lifetime (Ahmad et al., ). Cancer‐related morbidity and mortality can be reduced by routinely screening asymptomatic individuals (Loud & Murphy, ). Cancer screening aims to prevent the development of cancer, by finding pre‐cancerous abnormalities, or detect it at an earlier stage, when it is generally more treatable and survivable (Wender et al., ). Many countries provide screening for a range of cancer types (e.g. breast, cervical and colorectal cancers), via organized programmes (e.g. the UK) or opportunistically (e.g. the USA) (Miles et al., ). Despite the benefits of cancer screening, a sizeable proportion of eligible individuals do not participate, with numerous practical, social, emotional and psychological barriers identified among the general population (Young et al., ). In 2022 to 2023, coverage of cervical, breast and bowel cancer screening in England was approximately 69% (NHS Digital, ), 67% and 72% (Office for Health Improvement and Disparities, ), respectively. Disparities in cancer screening uptake (Young & Robb, ) include lower participation rates among people with diabetes (Bhatia et al., ; Parrent et al., ).

label: macro
text: Prader–Willi syndrome (PWS [MIM: 176270]) results from pathogenic alterations in the maternally imprinted PWS region on chromosome 15q11–q13. These alterations include paternal deletions of a 5–6 Mb segment, maternal uniparental disomy of chromosome 15, or imprinting defects affecting the regulatory region. In contrast, Schaaf–Yang syndrome (SYS [MIM: 615547])—a genetically and clinically related disorder—is caused by truncating mutations in MAGEL2, a gene located within the same chromosomal region. Both syndromes share a range of phenotypic features.

label: macro
text: The rapid increase in prescribing has been attributed in part to its use as an alternative to opioids for the management of chronic pain in the context of the overdose crisis and growing evidence challenging the safety and efficacy of long‐term opioid therapy for chronic pain. This has been further supported by clinical guidelines presenting gabapentin as a first‐line therapy for broadly indicated neuropathic pain, despite the United States Food and Drug Administration's rejection of gabapentin for additional pain conditions in the early 2000s. Since it became available as a generic drug in the early 2000s, the financial incentive to seek approval for additional indications is low. While off‐label pharmaceutical prescribing is not inherently harmful or unusual, it operates at a greater level of clinical uncertainty that makes weighing the potential risks and benefits of treatment more challenging. In the context of gabapentin, there are growing concerns around the rapidly expanded use of the drug in light of the inconsistent quality of evidence supporting its off‐label use, poorly demonstrated efficacy across unapproved indications and emergent safety concerns.

text: We additionally performed multivariable logistic regression using the same three model structures, with non‐achievement of the guideline‐recommended HbA1c target (< 7% [< 53 mmol/mol]) at end of follow‐up (i.e., final HbA1c ≥ 7%) as a binary outcome. To assess dose–response relationships, we categorized seasonal amplitude into quartiles based on log‐transformed values and analysed using the full model (Model 2). label: macro
text: Logistical challenges are central to this situation. A significant portion of animal biodiversity consists of tiny invertebrates, primarily arthropods, which represent what has been termed dark taxa, i.e., groups that are hyperdiverse, largely undescribed, and severely underrepresented in biodiversity inventories, yet with the potential to encompass a substantial fraction of the still unknown terrestrial biota (Guerra et al. ). Characterizing this diversity has been historically difficult due to the high numbers of specimens found in single bulk samples, their extensive taxonomical diversity and the shortage of taxonomic expertise (Cardoso et al. ; Cardoso and Leather ; Harvey et al. ). Consequently, traditional morphological and molecular approaches have proven inadequate and costly in addressing the current data shortfalls in invertebrate biodiversity (Meier et al. ), thereby limiting our capacity to document large‐scale biogeographic patterns and assess biodiversity status across regions. In recent decades, high‐throughput DNA sequencing (HTS) is increasingly being used for ecosystem characterization and monitoring, offering the potential for a much deeper understanding of global biodiversity (e.g., Bohan et al. ; Bush et al. ; Taberlet et al. ). HTS has opened two complementary avenues for documenting and analysing invertebrate biodiversity: bulk‐sample metabarcoding to characterize community samples (Taberlet et al. ) and specimen‐based megabarcoding (Srivathsan et al. ). Both methods significantly help to overcome existing limitations in filling key gaps in biodiversity data, particularly concerning dark taxa (Emerson et al. ), with direct relevance for conservation biogeography.

text: Interaction maps (Figure 5) illustrate the binding profiles of B2 and B8. Compound B2 establishes hydrogen bonds through the carbonyl oxygens O1 and O2 of the 1,4‐naphthoquinone ring with a δ‐hydrogen (Hδ) of Asn91 and Asn95 respectively (see Figure 5C for ligand atom identification). An additional polar interaction is formed between the selenoester oxygen, O3, and the other Hδ of Asn91. Besides these interactions, CH‐π contacts are observed between the enzyme and the ligand. The aromatic 1,4‐naphthoquinone ring interacts with Asn91 and Ile217 side chains, and the 3,5‐dimethoxybenzene ring of the selenoate substituent also displays these types of interactions with Gly166 and Glu87. Compound B8 presents a similar recognition mode to B2 at the topoisomerase II binding site, exhibiting the same pattern of hydrogen bonds and CH–π contacts between the 1,4‐naphthoquinone ring and the protein residues. In addition, CH‐π interactions are present between the 1,3‐benzodioxole aromatic system and the amino acids Ala167, Gly166 and Glu87. Neither ligand B2 nor B8 interacts with the magnesium ion. Table S1 lists the average distances for these interactions alongside their occupancy throughout the simulation trajectory, demonstrating the complex stability through the entire trajectory.

label: macro
text: Glutamate, the main excitatory transmitter in cerebral cortex (Conti and Weinberg ), mediates point‐to‐point synaptic transmission, but also spills out of the synaptic cleft of active excitatory synapses to neighboring synapses, thereby exerting modulating effects at farther sites (Isaacson ; Kullmann ; Farrant and Nusser ; Semyanov ; Herman et al. ; Reiner and Levitz ). Such modulation, not predictable on anatomical grounds, occurs at neighboring excitatory and inhibitory synapses through glutamate homo‐ and hetero‐receptors, respectively (Lujan et al. ; Pinheiro and Mulle ; Reiner and Levitz ).

label: macro
text: Hadrontherapy is an advanced radiotherapy technique that allows for the effective treatment of deep‐seated and radio‐resistant tumors, as well as tumors located near critical organs. These advantages are related to the use of charged particles, such as protons and heavier ions (i.e., carbon‐ions), to deliver the dose with higher spatial precision compared to conventional photon therapy. This feature is due to the physical characteristics of charged particles, which deposit most of their energy at the end of their path, resulting in the so called Bragg Peak (BP). This confined distribution allows to give less dose to the surrounding healthy tissues. Additionally, carbon‐ions exhibit higher linear energy transfer (LET) and relative biological effectiveness (RBE), offering further benefits for radio‐resistant tumors.

label: macro
text: S65487 is a potent and selective small molecule inhibitor of Bcl2, developed for the treatment of hematological malignancies through intravenous (IV) administration. Its elimination is mainly metabolic and excretion of metabolites is mainly biliary‐mediated, leading to negligible renal excretion. Hepatic clearance is mediated by CYP3A4 (70%), CYP2D6 (20%), and UGT (10%). Additionally, S65487 is a substrate of the efflux transporter P‐glycoprotein. The terminal half‐life was approximately 8 h at microdose level and ranged from 2 to 6 h at therapeutic doses. The compound exhibits a low mean unbound fraction of 3.34%. To study its activity, Bcl2/Bim complex disruption was used as a target engagement biomarker. As part of the development of S65487, different modeling approaches provided valuable information regarding dosing strategy.

label: macro
text: Breastfeeding is vital to the wellbeing of the mother and child (Gribble and Gallagher ; Hernandez‐Cordero and Perez‐Escamilla ), is recognised as a human right to enable an infants' healthy future (COAG Health Council ) and is crucial for optimal growth and development (World Health Organization & UNICEF ). The World Health Organisation recognises breastfeeding as the best source of nourishment for infants, and is vital to achieving the highest standard of health (World Health Organization ). Breastfeeding provides essential, irreplaceable nutrition to optimise a child's growth and development, enhances maternal wellbeing, and privileges infant‐mother bonding. The removal of an infant, a child under 2 years of age (Arabena ), from their mother has profound implications, from the disruption of breastfeeding to inhibiting mother‐infant attachment (Weaver et al. ). The opportunity for an infant to bond with their mother is crucial to the short, medium, and long‐term health of the infant (Marsh et al. ). Secure and early mother‐infant attachment, which breastfeeding strongly encourages, is important for future interpersonal skills, and is protective; the longer the duration of breastfeeding the greater maternal sensitivity towards her child beyond the infant‐toddler period (Weaver et al. ).

label: macro
text: Oral and written discourse skills are crucial for social participation and academic performance. Students are expected to demonstrate their knowledge in all subjects through reasoning in either oral or written form. These demands pose particular challenges for students with language, reading and writing difficulties, for example, dyslexia. Dyslexia is characterised by deficits in written language, specifically word decoding and spelling (Catts et al. ). However, evidence suggests children and adolescents with reading difficulties (word reading and/or reading comprehension difficulties) also show significantly lower performance on several measures of writing compared to both age‐matched and reading‐matched peers (Graham et al. ) and that dyslexia may be associated with broader language vulnerabilities in both the written and oral modality (Adlof and Hogan ). Previous research on individuals with dyslexia has focused more on the written modality, however and more on reading than writing problems, even though writing difficulties are common and often persist into adulthood (Berninger et al. ; Connelly et al. ). Writing places high demands on linguistic, cognitive and executive resources and weaknesses in these skills might consequently affect written text production, beyond difficulties with spelling (Berninger and Amtmann ).

text: Previous studies examining air pollution and perioperative outcomes are few, and often limited to specialized populations while almost exclusively using frequentist approaches. These approaches provide limited capacity to communicate uncertainty in clinically interpretable probabilistic terms. Bayesian hierarchical methods offer an alternative: they allow integration of prior knowledge, direct probabilistic interpretation of effect sizes, and flexible handling of complex models. Despite these advantages, Bayesian approaches remain underutilized in perioperative environmental epidemiology.

label: macro
text: Among the studies investigating the health effects of RF exposure are those evaluating its impact on the autonomic nervous system (ANS) in humans. Although relatively few studies have addressed this topic, the available findings remain inconsistent. Some studies have reported significant RF‐induced changes in ANS‐related parameters (Esen and Esen ; Misek et al. ; Parizek et al. ) while others have found no observable effect (Braune S. et al. ; Selmaoui et al. ; Wallace et al. ). Variability in reported effects of RF exposure on autonomic nervous system outcomes likely reflects substantial methodological heterogeneity across studies, including differences in participant characteristics, exposure parameters, and experimental conditions (Michelant and Selmaoui ). Studies using autonomic challenges or more controlled stimuli appear to have subtle effects (Esen and Esen ); however, such challenges may themselves influence autonomic responses and therefore represent potential sources of bias. These considerations highlight the need for carefully designed experiments to accurately assess RF‐related modulation of autonomic function and evaluate their potential risks and ensure the safety of individuals exposed to these frequencies, especially with the expanding deployment of 5 G networks.

label: micro
text: All interviews were coded using Nvivo software, and notes were taken throughout to record the researcher's thoughts. The coding process followed the stages outlined by CGT: initial coding; focused coding; and theoretical coding (Charmaz, ). Initial ‘line‐by‐line’ coding derived codes directly from the data. Focused coding then compared and merged initial codes until major themes became apparent (Charmaz, , p. 50). Selective theoretical coding was then performed deductively, based on the research questions and conceptual framework. It related focused codes to each other and then to the framework to put the ‘story back together’ (Glaser, , p. 72), and linked themes to the main categories of barriers, solutions, actions, and the participants' worldviews. The use of CGT enabled the creation of more than one core category and abductive reasoning permitted the use of predefined categories derived from the research questions, while all themes emerged from the data.

text: In conclusion, the 2‐pyrazoline‐5‐one derivative demonstrates pronounced anticancer activity in both luminal A (MCF‐7) and triple‐negative breast cancer (MDA‐MB‐231) cells. Our compound induces dose‐dependent cytotoxicity, promotes apoptosis, suppresses clonogenic survival, inhibits migration, and significantly upregulates Bax expression. The marked increase in Bax levels suggests the engagement of the intrinsic apoptotic pathway, indicating the activation of mitochondria‐mediated apoptotic signaling. This observation raises the possibility that this molecule may function as a Bax‐modulating or Bax‐activating agent. The present findings highlight this scaffold as a potential candidate for mitochondria‐targeted therapeutic strategies in breast cancer.

label: macro
text: The sheath‐liquid ce‐TWIMS‐MS platform enables robust and reproducible native analysis of intact protein complexes across a wide range of infusion flows while preserving structural fidelity. Across all conditions, SA tetramers retained native‐like conformations, with stable ATDs, CSDs, and Ω values, indicating minimal perturbation of the underlying gas‐phase structures. Infusion flow primarily modulated ion transmission and peak shape/abundance rather than inducing substantial conformational rearrangement. Low infusion flows enhanced detection of compact, low‐charge‐state populations with improved conformational resolution, whereas intermediate flows maximized overall signal intensity. These findings establish infusion flow rate as a tunable experimental parameter for sensitivity and structural selectivity in native IMS‐MS measurements. Altogether, this work demonstrates that the sheath‐liquid ce‐MS interface provides a stable and transferable front‐end for automated native TWIMS‐MS workflows. Furthermore, the ability to maintain controlled continuous flow may facilitate the direct analysis of protein complexes in non‐volatile buffers (e.g., PBS) via Taylor‐Aris dispersion. Such capability significantly expands the scope of native MS toward more biologically authentic environments.

label: macro
text: The first DTCs focused on rationalizing the supply of medi‐cines in the hospital setting. In Sweden, the first DTC was established at Karolinska University Hospital already in 1961, with the main task to decrease the use of medicines with poor documentation and/or low cost‐effectiveness. Over the years, the DTCs have broadened their focus. In 2013, a review of 207 articles described the evolution of DTCs as a consequence of changes in healthcare systems and the role of the prescriber. In Sweden, the typical DTC consists of physicians, pharmacists, and nurses, but the base from which members can be recruited has widened over time. The initial formularies have evolved from limited lists of medicines into complete systems of medication management policies intended to ensure safe, appropriate, and cost‐effective use of pharmaceuticals in the interface between hospitals, primary care, patients, and the general public.

label: macro
text: Reactive balance training (RBT) has shown promise for enhancing reactive balance control and reducing falls poststroke. However, the optimal training parameters (e.g., intensity, duration) are unknown. This secondary analysis examined dose–response relationships between specific reactive balance training characteristics and improvements in reactive balance control and fall rates poststroke. 30 people with chronic stroke (mean age: 66.6 ± 8.8 years; time after stroke: 3.3 ± 2.3 years) completed up to 12 one‐hour reactive balance training sessions, twice per week. Training included experiencing losses of balance due to internal or external perturbations while performing voluntary tasks. The tasks were of four types: stable, quasi‐mobile, mobile, and unpredictable, each with choice of three difficulty levels (normal, increased, or reduced). Multiple linear regression (reactive balance outcome) and negative binomial regression (fall rates) were used to model associations between training characteristics (total number of perturbations, task difficulty, perceived challenge, and success rate) and post‐training fall rates and changes in the reactive balance control sub‐score of the mini‐Balance Evaluation Systems Test (mini‐BESTest). A higher number of perturbations were significantly associated with better post‐intervention reactive balance scores on the mini‐BEST (p = 0.010). There were no significant associations with any other training characteristics and post‐intervention mini‐BEST Scores. For fall incidence rates, there were no significant associations with any training characteristic (all p > 0.05). Greater exposure to RBT was associated with improvements in reactive balance control among individuals with chronic stroke. Participants who completed a higher number of perturbations within the standardized 12‐session protocol showed greater improvements in reactive balance control, consistent with a dose–response association. These results indicate that training volume—reflected in the number of perturbations experienced—may be an important factor to consider when refining RBT protocols.

Although the m/z 315 fragment ion of nifedipine is only marginally more thermodynamically stable than that of m‐nifedipine (by 1.48 kcal/mol, Table 1), this small energy difference alone does not account for the pronounced differences observed in Figure 2. Instead, the results suggested that the observed fragmentation patterns are primarily governed by the kinetic stability of m/z 315 in nifedipine and its isomers.

label: macro
text: For health promotion, these findings highlight the importance of working with culturally embedded practices. Netto et al. argue that culturally responsive interventions must begin with the logics through which communities already understand and enact health. Incorporating structured discussions about home remedies, dietary routines and the selective use of Ayurveda into preventive care may support trust, continuity and understanding, thereby strengthening engagement in ways that align with salutogenic principles. The central role of seniors' clubs in participants' accounts further underscores the value of community settings as culturally safe, health‐supportive environments. Strengthening partnerships between health services and seniors' clubs, including the provision of culturally considerate, family‐centred health advice; intergenerational activities and digital media partnerships, may position these community settings as preventive health infrastructure rather than peripheral supports, enabling early, low‐threshold engagement with health information in ways that reflect how older migrants already seek advice and reassurance. However, as noted in the limitations of the study, for those who are socially isolated or disengaged from ethnic networks, stabilising a sense of coherence requires physical and social environments to be deliberately structured to promote salutogenic principles. Therefore, proactive service‐level responses, including outreach through primary care and culturally informed home visiting, may be necessary to ensure their health needs are not rendered invisible within community‐focused health promotion frameworks.

label: macro
text: Our ancestral niches vary in kinship intensity (Curtin et al. ). ST societies are those with intensive kinship, “characterized by extended family networks, cousin marriage, polygyny, endogamy, unilineal descent, and an increase in relatedness within kin groups” (p. 417). WT societies are those with extensive kinship that “tend to encourage the formation of broad social networks with unrelated individuals, and often feature exogamy and bilateral descent” (p. 417). Agriculture is the ancestral niche for hierarchical ST societies, whereas an extreme form of WT with weak and loose kin ties (Curtin et al. ) may trace its ancestral niche to Medieval Christianity (Schulz et al. ).

text: To empirically assess controllability models' entropy bias, we constructed a controllability‐based variant of our framework by replacing Equation 3 with a controllability heuristic: where  is the support of the action space and  is the entropy of outcome , which is derived through differential quantile entropy (Vasicek):where  is the derivative of the quantile function . For simulation, to challenge the causal model's capacity to detect non‐obvious action‐outcome relationships, we replaced the original unimodal outcome distribution  with a bimodal Gaussian mixture comprising two equally weighted components:  and . Unlike controllability models, which conflate causal influence with entropy reduction, our framework successfully identifies the causal link between limb movements and the emergence of bi‐modality—despite the outcome distribution's increased entropy. This demonstrates the model's ability to infer causality through distributional restructuring rather than stability‐seeking heuristics, generalizing to scenarios where actions induce complex, multimodal outcome dynamics.

label: macro
text: The hypothalamic–pituitary–thyroid axis plays a crucial role in regulating metabolism and reproduction, yet its age‐ and sex‐related dynamics in dromedary camels remain poorly understood. This study aimed to investigate age‐ and sex‐dependent variations in pituitary thyrotroph morphometry and thyroid hormone profiles in dromedary camels. A total of 90 clinically healthy Brela breed camels of both sexes (aged 2 to ≥11 years) were grouped based on age and physiological status (lactating and non‐lactating). Pituitary tissues (n = 60) were subjected to immunohistochemical analysis for thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH)‐producing thyrotrophs. Morphometric parameters, including cell count, size, and nuclear dimensions, were measured. Plasma concentrations of TSH, triiodothyronine (T3), and thyroxine (T4) were determined using ELISA. Thyrotroph cell counts did not differ significantly among groups. However, older camels (≥11 years) showed significantly increased cell and nuclear dimensions (p < 0.05). Plasma TSH levels decreased with age, with peak levels observed in females aged 5–10 years. T3 concentrations varied significantly with age and sex, with higher levels in younger males. T4 levels declined with age but were not significantly influenced by sex (p > 0.05). Thyrotroph morphology and thyroid hormone dynamics in dromedary camels are significantly influenced by age and sex, indicating adaptive modulation of the hypothalamic–pituitary–thyroid axis in response to metabolic and reproductive demands. This study demonstrates age‐ and sex‐dependent regulation of the hypothalamic pituitary thyroid axis in dromedary camels, showing enlarged thyrotroph morphology in older animals and distinct hormonal patterns, including declining T4 with age‐ and sex‐related variation in T3 and TSH levels.

section_name: Aim, Approach and the Case for Theoretical Intervention
label: micro
text: To develop this argument, the paper proceeds in four stages. It first revisits Dorothea Orem's self‐care theory to establish a conceptual baseline in which dependence and care‐seeking are ethically legitimate conditions rather than failures to be overcome. It then examines how neoliberal health discourse has reconfigured self‐care from capacity to obligation, and names the tension that follows as the self‐care paradox. The analysis then draws on social–theoretical resources, particularly accounts of the achievement society and the internalisation of performance demands, to illuminate how this paradox operates within the subject, as a self‐imposed foreclosure of care. Finally, it considers what this analysis asks of nursing theory: not a new model of self‐care, but a more honest reckoning with what nursing's own discourse does when it enters the interior lives of the people it exists to care for.

label: macro
text: Fabry disease (FD, OMIM 301500) is an X‐linked lysosomal storage disorder caused by deficient activity of lysosomal alpha‐galactosidase A (AGAL, E.C. 3.2.1.22) due to pathogenic variants in the GLA gene (HGNC:4296, Xq22.1). Plasmatic deacylated globotriaosylceramide (lysoGb3) is elevated in FD patients as a reflection of lysosomal accumulation of Gb3. Specific (AGALopathic) GLA variants have been recently shown to accumulate within the secretory pathway and trigger endoplasmic reticulum stress and unfolded protein response rather than result in profound enzymatic deficiency. In part due to lack of integrative measures of clinical severity and biochemical/molecular parameters, specific impacts and consequences of X‐chromosomal inactivation (XCI) on clinical manifestation in FD female heterozygotes still remain to be fully understood. Our study aimed at evaluation of XCI (% of inactive wt GLA allele) in untreated female FD heterozygotes with classic FD (n = 17), late‐onset FD (n = 19) and individuals carrying GLA variants (p.(L394P) (n = 7), p.(A143T) (n = 4), and p.(D313Y) (n = 4)) with predominant AGALopathic effects. XCI was correlated with age of the patients, clinical phenotype, (residual) AGAL activity, and lysoGb3. AGAL activity corresponded to XCI independently of the type of the GLA mutation. The best separation of the clinical phenotypes (classic FD, late‐onset FD and AGALopathy) was achieved by correlating XCI to the ratio of AGAL activity to lysoGb3. This three parametric calculated marker was then confronted with the Mainz Severity Score Index (MSSI) to generate an Integrative Clinical‐Laboratory quotient (ICLq). ICLq discriminated the three female patient groups and demonstrated group‐dependent differences in its average age‐related increase.

label: micro
text: The linear model provides a theoretical estimate of ion abundance as a function of spot size and is not constrained by detector sensitivity. As a result, the model predicts nonzero abundances even at spot sizes where experimental detection reaches zero, so measured cutoffs are dictated by limited analyte availability and instrument sensitivity rather than fundamental desorption or ionization efficiency. This approach allows quantification of the rate of signal loss with increasing spatial resolution. In Figure 5A, four representative lipids were chosen to illustrate the overall trend observed across most detected species: Ion abundance decreases as spatial resolution increases. The theoretical line hits zero only as spatial resolution nears the submicron range, whereas the experimental data reach zero abundance around 20 μm. This reflects detection limits rather than true molecular absence. At very small beam diameters, the desorbed plume contains fewer neutral molecules overall, reducing the total number of analytes that can reach the electrospray region. While a smaller number of neutrals could, in theory, lessen competition for charge and slightly improve ionization efficiency, the absolute number of ions generated still decreases because of the limited material available for post‐ionization. The lower plume density also reduces overlap with the electrospray, and the smaller ablation volume may lead to incomplete desorption. These effects cause the measured abundance to drop to zero more rapidly than predicted by the ideal theoretical model, emphasizing that spatial resolution improvements are ultimately constrained by both desorption volume and total analyte availability rather than ionization inefficiency alone.

label: macro
text: Rabbits are of great interest as pets and laboratory animals, especially in ophthalmological research (Ghaffari and Moghaddassi). However, these animals may become easily stressed and uncooperative, challenging ophthalmological examinations. As rabbits' mortality associated with general anaesthesia is high compared to dogs and cats (1.39% vs. 0.17% and 0.24%, respectively), sedatives can be used to reduce animals' stress and facilitate routine clinical diagnostic techniques (Aghababaei et al.; Bellini et al.; Yavuz et al.). Although sedation can induce adverse ophthalmic side effects, it has been documented that a combination of sedatives may minimize adverse effects (Aghababaei et al.; Di Pietro et al.; Karamichali et al.). As no sedative agent has yet met all the requirements of ophthalmic procedures, research continues to find an ideal sedation protocol with the least adverse effects on ocular parameters (Rastabi et al.).

text: The lead compounds with the highest cell growth inhibitory activity in MDA‐MB‐231 cells, compounds B2, B3, B4, B8, and B9 were submitted to the Developmental Therapeutics Program (DTP) of the National Cancer Institute (NCI). All the compounds exhibited remarkable results in the One‐Dose assay (1·10−5 M) against a panel of 55 cancer cell lines (Figures S120–S125) with compound B8 displaying the lowest mean value of cell growth (−91.86%), though the all the five compounds exhibited a negative mean value of cell growth with values between −15.51% and −91.86%. Based on these results, all five hit compounds advanced to the NCI Five‐Dose assay, yielding GI50  (50% growth inhibition), TGI (total growth inhibition), and LC50  (50% lethal concentration) values across various cancer cell lines from dose‐response curves. Data are shown in Figures S126–S130 and overviewed in Figure 2. All five compounds showed GI50 values in the low micromolar range with values below 12 µM. The hit compounds demonstrated more activity in the SR leukemia cell line and the HS 578 T breast cancer cell line, which are the most sensitive cell lines, including the melanoma cell lines. These findings support the hypothesis that the combination of 1,4‐naphthoquinone and selenium yields hybrid molecules with enhanced antitumor activity and potential for further development.

label: macro
text: Metabolic reprogramming is a defining hallmark of tumors. Among metabolic pathways, (Hanahan ) glutamine metabolism has attracted considerable attention because of its dual function as a carbon and nitrogen source, estalishing its role as a “carbo–nitrogen hub” (Cluntun et al. ). Through glutaminolysis, glutamine supplies α‐ketoglutarate to the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle to support bioenergetics and anabolic processes, while glutathione (GSH) and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) production to counter oxidative stress and preserve redox homeostasis (Gong et al. ; Jin et al. ). Tumors frequently exhibit pronounced glutamine dependence, or “glutamine addiction” (Quek et al. ; Zou et al. ). Aberrant reprogramming of glutamine metabolism not only sustains cancer cell proliferation and survival, but also drives tumor microenvironment (TME) remodeling, including activating cancer‐associated fibroblasts, enhancing immunosuppressive myeloid‐derived suppressor cell and Tregs functions, and promoting tumor‐supportive macrophage polarization (Zou et al. ; Ma et al. ; Hu et al. ). Consequently, glutamine metabolism maintains cellular homeostasis while shaping a microenvironment that fosters tumor migration and invasion, positioning it as a major metabolic driver of the metastatic cascade.

text: Likelihood ratio tests were used to determine the statistical significance of each fixed effect and covariate by comparing a model containing the effect of interest to an otherwise identical model with that effect removed. Resulting p‐values were corrected for multiple comparisons using the Benjamini‐Hochberg (Benjamini and Hochberg) false discovery rate procedure, applied separately across all omnibus FAU models and all omnibus vocal cue models. For transparency, all post‐hoc comparisons used to probe significant omnibus effects are reported without correction. All reported models satisfied convergence criteria. Analyses were conducted using the lme4 package (V. 1.1‐33; Bates et al., ) in R (V. 4.3.0; R Core Team ); the anova function contained in the stats package (V. 4.5.0; R Core Team) was used to derive model parameters. Significant interaction effects were probed using the emmeans package (V. 1.8.5; Lenth).

label: macro
text: Documentation of ADRs in the electronic medical record (EMR) is often limited, requiring identification to be done using a reaction phenotype rather than discretely coded events. To date, numerous strategies have been proposed for detection of rare ADRs, largely centered around utilization of computational tools and various data sources, including EMR, social media, and scientific literature. The accuracy of these approaches, however, is directly tied to the quality of underlying documentation. As rare ADRs are often underrecognized, EMR documentation is frequently inconsistent with a lack of clear causality between a drug and reaction. This is known to be driven in part by variability in medication exposure timelines as related to the reaction, reaction severity, or even clinical awareness that a rare ADR exists to a medication. Identification is further complicated in reactions with nonspecific characteristics, or those which occur outside of acute care settings. The confluence of such factors precludes automated identification of confirmed cases without a degree of clinical review.

label: macro
text: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) is a federal public assistance program which provides food benefits to low-income working people, seniors, people who are disabled and others to support their grocery budget. SNAP participation reduces food insecurity by up to 30% and has been shown to reduce poverty. Approximately 80% of those eligible for SNAP utilize the program and in 2022, SNAP served 12.3% of the US population. Yet, despite high utilization and participation, many SNAP participants remain food insecure. Furthermore, eligibility requirements restrict participation, particularly for non-citizens and immigrants who are undocumented. For example, non-citizens may be eligible for SNAP if they are refugees. Findings from a study examining food insecurity, immigration status, and SNAP participation found that SNAP utilization could effectively reduce disparities in food security status between immigrants and US-born citizens.

label: macro
text: As children grow, peers provide a primary context in which social experiences significantly shape self‐perception and interactions with others (Parker et al., ). Developmental research has increasingly examined how children's social experiences within the group shape their prosocial tendencies (Stoltz et al., ). In particular, previous studies have found a solid positive association between children's social status within the group and their prosociality—in terms of cooperation, helping and kindness towards others (Angelis & Pensini, ; Berger et al., ; Lin et al., ; Warden & MacKinnon, ). For example, van den Berg et al.  investigated the association between social status and social behaviour among fifth‐ and sixth‐graders, using peer‐ and teacher‐nomination methods (i.e. children's ratings of classmates' popularity likability and social behaviours; teachers' reports on children's peer status and social behaviours). They found a positive link between popularity and prosocial behaviour, as perceived by peers and teachers, and a negative association between popularity and reported aggressive behaviour. Warden and MacKinnon  examined this link among 9–10‐year‐olds, using children's self‐reports of their social status, and their ratings by their peers. They revealed that the children's social status was positively correlated with self‐reported prosocial behaviours (inclusion, helping, sharing and caring) and empathy towards others (the latter being evident only for girls).

label: macro
text: The present study highlighted the significant association between parental burnout, job burnout, and family functioning. In particular, the present data suggest that balanced family functioning mitigates the negative effects of job burnout on parental burnout. Interestingly, above a certain threshold, family functioning showed a buffering effect. These findings have important theoretical and practical implications. Theoretically, they support an ecological and systemic perspective on parental burnout, emphasizing the importance of contextual resources—such as family relationships—in shaping individual outcomes. Practically, they highlight the need for interventions that go beyond individual stress management, promoting relational functioning within families. Prevention strategies and interventions designed to enhance family functioning, implemented at both systemic and individual levels, may serve to alleviate burnout among parents. Mental health support and stress reduction programs could play a pivotal role in this context. Furthermore, recognizing the interrelated nature of job burnout and parental burnout is essential when developing family and work‐related policies. Offering comprehensive support to working parents may contribute to improved family functioning and a reduction in burnout.

label: macro
text: Data accumulated over the last two decades have demonstrated that astrocytes play key roles in the regulation of synaptic transmission and plasticity. This is due, among other mechanisms, to their capability to detect and regulate synaptic transmission by expressing receptors and releasing gliotransmitters, respectively. Importantly, in juvenile rats, astrocytes are able to detect glutamate release at the level of individual synapses through mGluR5 and consequently up‐regulate excitatory synaptic transmission efficacy through the release of purines. Whether this upregulation is still present in the adult brain is an open question. Using immunohistochemistry and RNAscope on fixed tissue, as well as electrophysiological recordings on acute hippocampal brain slices of adult male rats, we demonstrated that this regulatory pathway also prevails in adult rats. Most surprisingly, such facilitation of glutamate release that is readily observed when a small number of synapses are activated was completely abolished under conditions where a large number of inputs were stimulated. These findings thus suggest that astrocytes integrate the incoming afferent information and adapt their responses depending on the network activity. In adulthood, astrocytic mGluR5 increases synaptic efficacy through presynaptic A2ARs. This occurs when a small number of synapses are activated simultaneously. This pathway is abolished when numerous synapses are recruited.

label: macro
text: This study revealed that IMS within Team Orange in Sapporo is structured as a cyclical process encompassing diverse triggers for initiating matching, assessing the needs of PLWD and families, coordinating support with attention to safety and continuity, building trust relationships to facilitate matching, reaching consensus among PLWD, families and supporters, implementing IMS and reviewing and readjusting toward sustainable support. The novelty of this study lies in theorising Team Orange practice as a support process in which the agency of PLWD, families and supporters is centred, while the coordinators' clinical reasoning, relational coordination and risk management cyclically underpin this agency. Our findings suggest that the IMS can be positioned as a community‐based integrated care practice model that safely organises informal support within an institutional framework, continuously connecting local human resources to address individual challenges not fully met by formal services, thereby bridging formal and informal support systems.

label: macro
text: Private mitigation actions play a role in reducing wildfire risks to individual parcels and creating resilient communities. A substantial body of research shows that homeowners' perceptions of wildfire risk on their parcels are one important factor, among many others, that influences mitigation behavior (Brenkert‐Smith et al.; Champ et al.; Cowan and Kennedy; Dickinson et al.; I. M. Martin et al.; W. E. Martin et al.; McFarlane et al.; McNeill et al.; Nelson et al.; Talberth et al.). Therefore, when a gap exists between homeowners' and assessors' perceptions of parcel‐level risk, communities could be left underprepared. These gaps in risk perceptions can have serious consequences as households that underestimate their risk may not invest in critical mitigation measures or prepare for evacuation.

label: macro
text: In this study, the impact of scatter correction methods on SPECT images of the recently developed radiopharmaceutical 161Tb was assessed qualitatively and quantitatively. Our findings indicate that both DEW and TEW correction methods improve spatial resolution by sharpening object boundaries and increase CNR by reducing scattering contributions, although with a slight reduction in SNR due to decreased total counting statistics. The TEW approach outperformed the DEW approach in terms of quantitative accuracy under complicated scattering conditions (water with/without activity). In SPECT imaging using therapeutic amounts of Tb‐161, high image quality can be achieved with an energy window of 74.6 keV ± 10%.

label: macro
text: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is a major contributor to the global burden of noncommunicable diseases, affecting an estimated 850 million people worldwide and imposing substantial morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs  . The burden is disproportionately concentrated in low- and lower-middle-income countries (LICs and LMICs), where access to early diagnosis, preventive strategies, and renal replacement therapies, including dialysis and transplantation, is limited  . Kidney transplantation is the most cost-effective treatment for end-stage renal disease (ESRD) and offers superior survival and quality of life compared with dialysis  . However, organ shortage results in prolonged waiting times and inequitable access  . Immunological barriers, particularly human leukocyte antigen (HLA) incompatibility and sensitization, further restrict access to transplantation  .

Investigating the perceptions of vegan men in the general population may help inform policy and practice in mental health care for vegan men.

label: macro
text: Young people must be central to health response design to ensure they are relevant and effective. It requires a clearer understanding of the opportunities and expectations surrounding youth engagement. Therefore, this study aims to gain a deeper understanding of the health priorities of young adults residing in South Australia (SA) and their engagement in the design of health programmes. In‐depth interviews were conducted with 13 young adults aged 18–24 years (all genders) in SA to identify priority health concerns, contributing factors and preferred solutions. We also explored their interest and expectations of being involved in the health programme design. Thematic analysis was conducted. Mental health and vaping emerged as the predominant health concerns. Key drivers of health concern amongst young adults included a lack of awareness of vaping and eating disorders, peer pressure, living costs, poor work‐life balance and limited mental health services. Participants recommended early, school‐based interventions, and free accessible services. They underscored the need for structural changes at policy‐level to create supportive environments. Participants expressed interest in contributing to intervention design when aligned with their interests, skills and availability, but reported limited opportunities. Young adults are eager to be involved in health programme design and have unique skills that should be better leveraged upon through co‐design of research and programmes. Effective solutions to pressing health concerns must align with the lives of young adults and actively involve them in the design, implementation and evaluation of programmes and policies. The study results suggest employing the motivation and insights of young adults can lead to more relevant, effective and engaging health solutions tailored to their needs.

label: macro
text: Chemical exchange saturation transfer (CEST) magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is an emerging molecular imaging technique that enables noninvasive detection of endogenous metabolites and macromolecules. It offers valuable insights into key physiological processes: for example, amide CEST, originating from peptides and proteins, can be used to detect muscular abnormalities in diabetic foot or to monitor pH variations in skeletal muscles; creatine CEST (around 2 ppm), primarily reflecting creatine (Cr) and phosphocreatine (PCr), serve as a potential biomarker for mitochondria function and energy metabolism, which is particularly relevant in conditions such as sarcopenia, disuse atrophy, or metabolic myopathy; relayed nuclear Overhauser effect (rNOE), which is sensitive to mobile lipids and proteins, can provide insight into lipid–protein interactions and may serve as an early indicator of adiposity‐related metabolic remodeling; and magnetization transfer (MT), originating from semisolid macromolecules, offers a surrogate for tissue structural integrity that can be altered in myopathies. Owing to these versatile capabilities, CEST MRI holds considerable potential for probing metabolic and compositional heterogeneity within skeletal muscles.

text: We next explored the primary deuterium kinetic isotope effects of these reactions using a commercially available per‐deuterated substrate (D31‐LA) and a synthetic di‐deuterated (11,11‐D2‐LA) substrate (Figure S2a). The D k cat was determined by dividing the first‐order rate constants for the natural abundance substrate by that of the deuterated substrate. For reference, the GmLOX1 reaction revealed a D k cat of ~70 at 25°C and pH 9, which is in good agreement with reported literature values (Glickman et al., ; Hwang & Grissom, ). The D k cat values for the other plant LOXs follow a similar trend, in the range of ~40–60. These values indicate that the chemical step is rate‐limiting for the first‐order rate constant. It is important to note that the Km for the deuterated substrate was elevated in the 9‐LOXs. For example, the Km of D31‐LA for the AtLOX1 reaction was found to be ca. 20 μM (Figure S2c). The use of the di‐deuterated substrate at the reactive carbon (11,11‐D2‐LA) resulted in nearly identical kinetic properties to the perdeuterated substrate (cf. Figure S2c and Table 2). Thus, the additional deuterium labels did not greatly influence the kinetic parameters. This overall behavior is distinct from the GmLOX1 reaction, in which the Km value of the deuterated substrate (7 μM at 25°C; see Table 2) is reduced from the natural abundance (22 μM).

text: Natural compounds and their synthetic derivatives have long served as valuable sources for anticancer drug discovery (Atanasov et al. ; Deng et al. ; El‐Seedi et al. ; Naeem et al. ). Quinones, widely present throughout the natural world, constitute a privileged chemotype for the creation of innovative antitumor compounds (McClendon and Osheroff ; Prasad et al. ). In particular, 1,4‐naphthoquinone, a secondary metabolite found in plants, marine invertebrates, fungi, and bacteria, is recognized for its cytostatic and antibiotic properties (Kamarudin et al. ; Widhalm and Rhodes ). This scaffold is widely used in pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and chemical industries, and is found in various natural and synthetic compounds with therapeutic activities including anticancer (Ivasechko et al. ; Tan et al. ), antifungal (Chaves‐Carballo et al. ), antiplatelet (Kuo et al. ; Monroy‐Cárdenas et al. ), and antibacterial (Liu et al. ; Ravichandiran et al. ; Yap et al. ), among others (Aminin and Polonik ; de Araújo et al. ; Navarro‐Tovar et al. ; Paengsri et al. ).

label: macro
text: Glutamate spillover from excitatory synapses modulates neighboring inhibitory synapses, yet the ultrastructural organization of the major glutamate transporter GLT‐1 at these sites remains poorly defined. Using quantitative pre‐embedding electron microscopy in rat and human cortex, we found that GLT‐1‐positive astrocytic leaflets (ALs) were frequently juxtaposed to morphologically identified symmetric synapses, with similar prevalence across axo‐somatic, proximal axo‐dendritic, and distal axo‐dendritic subtypes. Because inhibitory synapses are embedded in a dense excitatory neuropil, we applied distance‐based phenotyping relative to the nearest asymmetric synapse to define symmetric‐associated GLT‐1+ ALs. Within this population, distal axo‐dendritic symmetric synapses showed shorter AL‐to‐synaptic‐edge distances and were embedded in a tighter local excitatory microenvironment. Post‐embedding immunogold further showed that GLT‐1 was enriched at the plasma membranes of ALs and localized extrasynaptically relative to symmetric synapses. Consistently, symmetric‐associated membrane GLT‐1 and closely spaced GLT‐1/α2 couples (with an interdistance ≤ 50 nm) were preferentially localized within 1000 nm of distal symmetric synapses compared to proximal. Similar organizational features of membrane GLT‐1/α2 couples were observed in human cortex. These findings identify a subtype‐dependent extrasynaptic astrocytic GLT‐1 organization at cortical inhibitory synapses and provide a morphological framework for glutamate‐dependent modulation of inhibitory signaling. GLT‐1+ ALs frequently juxtapose symmetric synapses. Distance‐based phenotyping identifies symmetric‐ and asymmetric‐associated GLT‐1+ ALs. Distal inhibitory synapses show enriched GLT‐1/α2 couples in ALs within 1000 nm, conserved in human cortex.

label: macro
text: Research has underscored the interconnection between work and family domains, showing that an imbalance between demands and resources can lead to job and parental burnout. Our study examined the moderating role of family functioning in the association between job burnout and parental burnout among working parents in Italy. Drawing on Olson's Circumplex Model, balanced family functioning was conceptualized as optimal levels of cohesion and flexibility, reflecting a well‐regulated family dynamic. Three‐hundred‐nine working parents of children aged 0–18 completed a survey assessing sociodemographic characteristics, job burnout, parental burnout, and family functioning. Results indicate that parental burnout was positively related to job burnout and negatively to family functioning. Simple slope analyses showed that the association between job and parental burnout progressively weakened as family functioning increased and became non‐significant beyond a specific threshold. Our findings highlight the clinical value of balanced family functioning's buffering effect on work–family stress processes.

text: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) is a clinically and molecularly heterogeneous disease. PKMYT1, a G2/M cell cycle kinase, has been implicated in tumor progression in several cancers, but its role in CLL remains unclear. We evaluated PKMYT1 expression in primary CLL samples and analyzed associations with cytogenetic features and clinical parameters. PKMYT1 expression was heterogeneous and correlated with adverse features, including complex karyotype and elevated leukocyte counts. Comparative transcriptomic analyses between high‐ and low‐expression groups revealed enrichment of pathways related to chromatin remodeling, DNA repair, and mitotic regulation. In MEC1 cells, pharmacological PKMYT1 inhibition significantly reduced cancer cell viability. PCR analysis showed upregulation of TP53, CASP3, BAK, GSDMD, BCL2, CASP1, IL1β, RIPK1, and RIPK3, indicating activation of apoptotic, inflammasome‐associated, and necroptotic pathways. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that PKMYT1 is heterogeneously expressed in CLL, associated with adverse cytogenetic and clinical features, and critical for cell survival, highlighting its potential as a therapeutic target.

text: Prior to the main analyses, preliminary analyses examined correlations between background variables (e.g., infant sex, birth weight, gestational age, SES) and the study's variables of interest. SES was significantly correlated with child externalizing and internalizing problems at 5.5 years of age; specifically, higher SES was associated with lower externalizing and internalizing problems. Therefore, SES was controlled for in the moderated mediation analyses. To account for age at assessment, child age at each assessment was regressed on the relevant variables, and the resulting residuals were used in the analyses. Moderated mediation analyses were conducted using bias‐corrected bootstrap sampling with 5000 iterations (Hayes,), which is considered the preferred approach for hypothesis testing in small sample sizes (MacKinnon,). Consistent with standard recommendations for bootstrap estimation, the confidence interval of the effect was used as the primary and most robust indicator of statistical significance. Effects were considered significant if the corresponding bootstrap confidence interval did not include zero (Hayes,).

label: macro
text: Lyon and coworkers demonstrated that multiple random fragmentation events can bias intensity distributions toward smaller terminal fragment ions. With any high‐yield method of dissociation, such as high‐energy collisional activation, this phenomenon can greatly influence the intensity distribution of product ions in MS/MS spectra. Previously, multiple fragmentation events were simulated with computationally intensive stochastic studies. The goal of this work is to provide a mathematical model that explains the results obtained from computational stochastic studies. We present a probabilistic model that predicts terminal and internal product ion intensities based on polypeptide size and the number of fragmentation events. This model is validated by demonstrating convergence with the previous stochastic model and described that the intensity trend from smaller fragments to larger fragments follows the probability of multiple fragmentation events on peptide backbone. Under the stated assumptions, the analytical expressions formally demonstrate that the “missing middle” is a necessary mathematical consequence of multiple fragmentation events, and they reproduce the stochastic simulation results with exact agreement while reducing computation time from hours to less than 1 s. Our method consistently offers greater accuracy and mathematically proves the mechanistic necessity of “missing middle” phenomenon in top‐down MS. The abundances of fragments from stochastic simulation distribute around the average value calculated by the probability formula within twofold coefficient variance (%CV). The closed‐form probability expressions accurately describe the combinatorial consequences of multiple fragmentation events under the stated assumptions. Because they are computationally inexpensive and differentiable, these expressions could be integrated with sequence‐dependent fragmentation propensities in future machine learning models for MS/MS spectral prediction.

label: macro
text: Infrared matrix‐assisted laser desorption electrospray ionization (IR‐MALDESI) mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) enables label‐free, spatially resolved molecular analysis of biological tissues under ambient conditions. While improving spatial resolution is important for mapping fine tissue structures, mid‐infrared (IR) lasers are diffraction limited to laser spot sizes greater than ~5 μm, necessitating creative approaches to enhance spatial detail for subcellular MSI. An adjustable, ring‐actuated iris combined with a reflective objective was integrated into the IR‐MALDESI MSI platform, replacing the conventional beam expander and collimator assembly. Laser beam diameter on target was systematically varied, and ion abundances were measured using high‐resolution mass spectrometry. Normalized ion abundances were fitted to a linear model as a function of beam diameter to predict signal at increasing spatial resolutions. Ion abundance decreased linearly with decreasing laser beam diameter across the tested range. Linear regression of normalized ion abundances enabled prediction of signal levels at progressively higher spatial resolutions. The tunable aperture provided stable beam control and reproducible ablation, allowing quantitative assessment of sensitivity trade‐offs associated with reduced laser spot size. Controlled beam restriction using an adjustable iris enables fine tuning of spatial resolution in IR‐MALDESI MSI by deliberately discarding a large fraction of high laser power while maintaining measurable ion abundance. Far‐field diffraction and tissue ablation thresholds confine material removal beyond simple geometric scaling, and linear modeling of ion abundance versus beam diameter enables quantitative prediction of signal loss at high spatial resolution.

text: Pharmaceuticals play an essential role in reducing mortality and morbidity. However, if they are improperly prescribed or used, the negative consequences can outweigh their benefits. In 1985, the World Health Organization (WHO) launched the concept of rational use of medicines, advocating that “all patients should receive medication appropriate to their medical needs, in doses meeting their own individual requirements, for an adequate period of time and at the lowest costs to them and the community”. Drug and Therapeutics Committees (DTCs) were suggested as a suitable model to promote rational use of medicines, and WHO proposed that they should “ensure that patients are provided with the best possible cost‐effective and quality of care through determining what medicines will be available, at what cost, and how they will be used”.

Three values of k are noteworthy: a couple pattern (k = 1) where the actor and partner effects are equal in size; an actor‐only pattern (k = 0) where the partner effect is zero; and a contrast pattern (k = −1) where the actor and partner effects are equal in magnitude but differ in sign (Kenny and Ledermann ). When two Pi peaks were observed, both peaks were fit, and a weighted average of the two peaks was calculated. When calculating ratios of PDE/ATP and PCr/ATP, γ‐ATP was used to reflect relative ATP concentration. Muscle pH was calculated by the chemical shift between Pi relative to PCr. A mono‐exponential model was used to estimate the time constant (tau) of PCr recovery (PCrτ). Only data sets with a PCr to noise ratio of at least 10:1 at rest were used for analysis. In addition, if there was excessive movement artifacts resulting in inconsistent chemical shift position of peaks or linewidth then spectra were also excluded from analysis.

section_name: Introduction: The Invisible Labour of the Operating Room
label: macro
text: Something is consistently absent from accounts of perioperative practice. The safety literature attends to checklists, handovers and communication failures. The workforce literature counts hours, vacancies and attrition rates. The quality literature tracks infection rates, surgical outcomes and patient satisfaction scores. What these frameworks share is a studied inattention to the emotional dimension of perioperative work, to the labour of managing one's own feeling states in an environment that generates them continuously and intensely.

label: macro
text: Sexual dimorphism is increasingly recognised in biology and has been reported in cancer, with breast cancer (BC) showing the biggest dichotomy. While BC is still rare in males, the numbers receiving a BC diagnosis have increased globally from 8,500 diagnoses in 1990 to 23,100 in 2017, with age‐standardised rates in 100,000 person years of 0.46 and 0.61, respectively. There is evidence that increased obesity aligns with this increase. That we are an ageing population may also contribute, particularly to reduced overall survival (OS), but this does not account for the lack of improvement in BC‐specific survival observed for men diagnosed with stage III or IV BC over the past 30 years.

label: macro
text: High‐resolution structures of VDAC in the open conformation revealed a 19‐strand β‐barrel architecture containing an N‐terminal α‐helix positioned within the pore lumen (Bayrhuber et al., ; Hiller et al., ; Ujwal et al., ). In contrast, the structure of the closed state has yet to be determined, but early models proposed that voltage gating involves large‐scale displacement or partial unfolding of the N‐terminal segment. However, electrophysiological measurements and crosslinking studies have predominantly disfavored this mechanism, instead implicating structural plasticity of the β‐barrel itself, including elliptical deformation, as a contributor to gating (Teijido et al., ; Zachariae et al., ). Consistent with this view, recent molecular‐dynamics simulations indicate localized β‐strand flexibility rather than wholesales displacement of the N‐terminal helix (Ngo et al., ). While large N‐terminal ejection from the pore is unlikely, smaller rearrangements, such as helix rotation or subtle barrel deformation, remain possible and may occur either independently or in concert. Discriminating between N‐terminal rearrangement and β‐barrel deformation therefore remains a central challenge, particularly because the closed state is transient, sparsely populated, and typically accessed only under perturbative conditions such as applied voltage, reduced pH, mechanical stress, or interaction with partner proteins (Colombini, ;  Rostovtseva et al., ; Teijido et al., ).

label: macro
text: Baird's beaked whales are specialized deep‐diving predators that forage mainly on benthic and benthopelagic fishes from the deep sea, as well as a diverse assemblage of pelagic squid species. Their diet is dominated by gadiform fishes, including codlings (Moridae) and grenadiers (Macrouridae), alongside cephalopods from the families Gonatidae and Cranchiidae. There are no published data on the stomach contents of Sato's beaked whales, as the species was grouped together with Baird's beaked whale in earlier studies.

label: macro
text: The temporally weighted matching law (TWML; Shahan, ) has been recently proposed as the best model to account for the extinction burst (Fisher et al., ). The TWML proposes that extinction bursts emerge due to the release of competition from reinforcement‐related behavior when extinction begins. According to this model, the high reinforcement rate characteristic of small‐ratio schedules is responsible for these bursts, as extinction removes a substantial amount of reinforcement‐related behavior, temporarily reallocating that time to the target behavior (Fisher et al., ; Shahan, ). Thus, this reallocation effect is expected to be particularly pronounced under a fixed‐ratio 1 (FR 1) or similarly dense reinforcement schedule. Dense schedules typically yield a pattern consisting of one (or a small number of) target response followed by many reinforcement‐related behaviors (e.g., one lever press followed by several head entries into the hopper to retrieve the food pellet), whereas leaner (ratio) schedules are more likely to produce a higher number of target responses relative to reinforcement‐related behaviors, which in absolute terms should remain relatively constant across reinforcement schedules. For example, the extinction burst appears to be more common under small‐ratio schedules (e.g., Harris et al., ; Niyuhire et al., ; Pushparaj et al., ; Shahan & Avellaneda, ); however, although some studies have indirectly reported extinction burst prevalences under a FR 1 schedule, very few have examined extinction bursts as a primary focus under such a schedule (e.g, Katz & Lattal, ; Nist & Shahan, ).

label: micro
text: In the Waters Synapt G2Si instrument, the ion mobility cell is positioned between two collision cells, known as the trap cell and the transfer cell. This configuration allows the IMS separation of fragment ions formed prior to the IMS cell, as well as MS/MS analysis of ions exiting the IMS cell. If fragmentation occurs prior to the IMS (CID‐IMS), that is, within the trap cell, both precursor and fragment ions are separated by the travelling wave based on differences in their mobility and/or ATD. As an example, the fragments of nifedipine are separated by IMS, each exhibiting a distinct ATD, as shown in Figure 8a. Because all fragments are separated by IMS, the mass information within ion packet of the total ion mobility spectrum mainly corresponds to individual fragment ions (Figure 8b).

label: macro
text: Renal tumours pose an increasing health economic burden due to the rising incidental detection of localised renal masses, which present a diagnostic dilemma as they often remain indeterminate on initial evaluation due to the low specificity of current clinical imaging methods. This diagnostic uncertainty may result in unnecessary surgery, with the potential loss in renal function for those patients undergoing inappropriate nephrectomy. Conversely, a long‐term active surveillance strategy with imaging may be more appropriate than surgical resection for many patients, but this can affect patient mortality and morbidity if the detection of a malignant tumour is delayed and equally can result in psychological morbidity for patients with benign disease as they await a diagnosis. Renal mass biopsy prior to the decision for surgery can aid in the identification of benign cases, but misdiagnosis may result due to the challenges of accurately sampling small lesions, particularly in the context of tumour heterogeneity. Novel non‐invasive imaging techniques could aid the characterisation of renal masses on a whole‐tumour level.

label: macro
text: Although CME was long envisioned as a uniform process producing small, rapidly budding coated pits, high‐resolution imaging and ultrastructural studies have revealed a broader spectrum of clathrin‐coated assemblies at the plasma membrane. In addition to canonical clathrin‐coated pits, cells can form larger, flatter and longer‐lived clathrin lattices, commonly referred to as clathrin plaques. These plaques are particularly prominent at adherent cell surfaces and display distinct dynamics, spatial organization and dependencies on the actin cytoskeleton compared with classical pits. Recognition of this structural and dynamic heterogeneity has refined mechanistic models of CME, emphasizing the plasticity of clathrin coat architecture and the interplay between membrane mechanics, cytoskeletal forces and endocytic function ().

label: macro
text: Conventional radiotherapy treatments predominantly employ photon beams to deliver high doses to tumors, to eradicate malignant cells. Compared with photons, charged particles offer the advantage of a finite penetration depth, which can reduce dose deposition in healthy tissues distal to the tumor. Among clinically used charged particles, electrons are inexpensive to accelerate compared with heavier particles such as protons, as they can be produced using relatively compact linear accelerators, whereas proton therapy requires larger accelerator facilities. Conventional linear accelerator based electron therapy uses electron energies between 4 and 22 MeV and is therefore mainly restricted to the treatment of superficial targets due to their limited range of only a few centimeters.

label: macro
text: FAD/NAD(P)H‐dependent dehydrogenases form a structurally conserved family of redox enzymes that participate in essential metabolic processes across parasites and higher organisms. Among them, trypanothione reductase (TR) is a key component of the redox metabolism of Leishmania species and represents an attractive target for antileishmanial drug development. However, because several flavoproteins share similar folds and cofactor‐binding architectures, the selectivity of TR inhibitors remains a critical issue during early drug discovery. To explore this aspect, we investigated the structural landscape of FAD/NAD(P)H‐dependent dehydrogenases in Leishmania infantum by integrating sequence analysis, structural modeling, docking simulations, and in vitro biochemical validation. Reciprocal sequence searches revealed 11 parasite flavoproteins structurally related to TR, including a dihydrolipoamide dehydrogenase (DLD)‐like protein, a type II NADH dehydrogenase (NDH2)‐like protein, and a dienoyl‐CoA reductase (deCoAR)‐like protein. Comparative docking analyses across parasite and mammalian homologs allowed us to examine potential cross‐reactivity patterns among these enzymes. Enzymatic assays performed on recombinant L. infantum TR (LiTR) and Caldalkalibacillus thermarum NDH2 (CtNDH2) confirmed that selected ligands can exert enzyme‐dependent effects. In particular, auranofin and, to a lesser extent, nitrofurazone inhibited LiTR, whereas a terpyridine–Pt‐derived compound strongly inhibited LiTR while stimulating CtNDH2 activity. Overall, the results illustrate how structurally related flavoproteins may accommodate common ligands while responding with distinct catalytic outcomes. The integrated computational and biochemical workflow presented here provides a practical framework for assessing ligand selectivity within the FAD/NAD(P)H‐dependent dehydrogenase family and may support the development of selective modulators targeting parasite redox metabolism.

label: macro
text: Food security is the adequate access at all times to enough food for an active, healthy life. Conversely, food insecurity, which affects 12.8% of households in the United States, is the household-level economic and social condition of limited or uncertain access to adequate food. Food insecurity disproportionately affects people of color and disparities have increased since the COVID-19 pandemic. Food insecurity in the United States is rooted in poverty and systems of oppression, like income inequality and racism. As a result, food insecurity disproportionately impacts Black and Latine people compared to White people (22.4% and 20.8%, respectively, vs. 9.3%). Data also suggest that immigrants, or people who are foreign-born, are 1.28 times more likely to experience food insecurity than US-born citizens, perhaps due to income instability and lack of access to federal nutrition programs. Food security is a public health concern as it is a contributor to many health inequities, including chronic disease outcomes. For example, research has found a significantly increased risk of several diet-related diseases, including hypertension and hyperlipidemia, among low-income people experiencing food insecurity.

text: A five‐pool double‐step multi‐pool Lorentzian fitting (DMPLF) approach was applied, accounting for direct water saturation (DS) at 0 ppm, PCr at 2.5 ppm, amide at 3.5 ppm, rNOE at −3.5 ppm, and MT at −2.5 ppm:Unlike conventional multi‐pool Lorentzian fitting (MPLF) that fits all pools simultaneously, the DMPLF method employs a two‐step fitting strategy to improve parameter estimation robustness. In the first step, the background pools (DS, MT, and rNOE) were fitted using Z‐spectral data points outside the region of interest (ROI) for CEST pools, i.e., excluding the frequency range of 1–6 ppm. In the second step, the line shape parameters (amplitude, linewidth, and center frequency) of the background pools were fixed, and the amide and PCr peaks were fitted using the whole range of the Z‐spectra. This two‐step strategy reduces inter‐parameter interference and has been shown to yield more robust fitting results compared to conventional one‐step multi‐pool Lorentzian fitting. The initial guesses and fitting boundaries for all pools are summarized in Table 2.

text: The aim of this study is to investigate the association between single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) of porcine complement receptor 1‐like (CR1‐like) gene and its mRNA expression.
